summary: Drew DeVault's June 2022 status update opens by acknowledging the overwhelming supportive response to his earlier 'bleh' post about burnout. He reports deliberately shifting focus toward experimental and fun projects over routine maintenance. The bulk of his work went into the Helios microkernel, which saw major progress on capability-oriented design, userspace drivers, and syscall infrastructure. He also highlights advances in Himitsu (a secret store with a working Firefox add-on) and Hare (cross-compilation, complex numbers, date/time improvements). The update reflects a conscious decision to prioritize personal fulfillment in programming over obligation-driven work.
key_points: DeVault received hundreds of supportive emails after his burnout post, reflecting the strong personal connection his audience has with his work

The Helios microkernel saw rapid progress: capability-oriented refactoring, userspace drivers for serial and VBE graphics, interrupt handling, and a new syscall ABI — all within three months of the project's existence
Himitsu gained a working Firefox add-on, a new GTK+ prompter in Python, key deletion consent prompts, and progress toward full-disk encryption support
Hare received cross-compilation support, complex number support, and Martian time fixes in date/time handling
He explicitly chose fun experimental projects over routine SourceHut maintenance as a response to burnout
money_quotes: I have actually had a lot more fun programming this month than usual, since I decided to spend more time on experimental and interesting projects and less time on routine maintenance or long-term developments.
So, the feature you've been waiting for in SourceHut might be delayed, but in return, there's cool progress on the projects that you didn't even know you were waiting for.
The kernel is in a much better state now than it was a month ago (and to think it's only three months old!).
Of course, the SourceHut workload never dips below a dull roar, as I have to attend to business matters and customer support promptly.
I initially tried to provide these with thoughtful replies, then shorter replies, then I had to stop replying at all, but I did read every one.
key_insight: Deliberately prioritizing experimental passion projects over obligation-driven maintenance work can be a productive response to burnout, yielding rapid progress on ambitious systems like microkernels and programming languages.
